summary: Steve Yegge announces his departure from Grab after 2.5 years, citing the unsustainable vampire schedule required to work remotely with Asia during COVID. He reflects on Grab's strong social mission, engineering maturity, and unique Southeast Asian work culture that taught him valuable lessons about trust-based collaboration and alignment. The post details the 'meeting before the meeting' approach and how Western assumptions about directness are anti-patterns in Asian business culture. He closes by announcing his next adventure: finally launching his 25-year passion project, the MMORPG Wyvern, on Steam.
key_points: Remote work with Asia from Seattle required nonstop meetings from 1pm to 3am, ultimately proving unsustainable without in-person travel
Grab's collaboration culture runs on trust relationships built through personal time and drinks - without trust, you won't get things done
The 'meeting before the meeting' is the primary tool for getting alignment in Asian business culture - never surprise anyone, never call anyone out, get consensus beforehand
Western practices like vocal self-criticism and 'relentlessly exposing reality' are anti-patterns in Asian companies
Wyvern, his MMORPG passion project of 25 years, has a proven profit formula but suffers from a brutal learning curve that loses 99% of new players
money_quotes: My boss Mark Porter has straight-up taken all the mirrors out of his workspace and wears a black cape and fangs and he visits Romania and sleeps in a luxurious padded coffin down in his basement by day, rising with the moon for all-night meetings with Grab.

The first thing to know is that Grabbers are nice. They are nice to each other, and they value being respectful to each other, to a degree that most US companies can only aspire to as they send out memos asking people please not to be assholes.

If you don't have a trust relationship with all your stakeholders — typically something that can only be built by having drinks with them and spending a lot of personal time — then you won't get stuff done.
Don't ever bring solutions to problems, since they tend to alarm people. Just bring your problems!
The meeting itself should ideally be a rubber-stamp on the proceedings.
It takes humility to become a better person, and Grab is a humbling place.
25 years is a decently long development cycle, and Wyvern, despite appearing to have been designed by a third grader, is actually rich, complex, deep, and powerfully addictive.
key_insight: Effective leadership in cross-cultural environments requires abandoning Western assumptions about directness and building trust relationships through personal time, pre-alignment, and never surprising stakeholders in meetings.
